content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


December 5, 2001

Kirk Alan Pessner
Law Office of Russell H. Miller
20 Park Road, Suite E
Burlingame, CA 94010-4443

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-249

Dear Mr. Pessner:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the campaign reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Will use of your firm's office at the request of a local ballot measure committee for a phone bank supporting the ballot measure constitute a reportable contribution to the committee?

CONCLUSION

Yes. Such a use constitutes a reportable contribution if the ballot measure committee does not provide full and adequate consideration for use of the office.

FACTS

Your firm was asked by a local ballot measure committee to permit the committee to use your office for several nights to make local telephone calls to solicit support for a city measure. Your offices were subsequently used as a phone bank by volunteers of the committee. The volunteers phoned and asked voters if they supported the measure and, if they did, whether the voters would vote absentee. The voters were also asked if they would allow the use of their name in newspaper ads that the ballot measure committee ran. No

_______________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-01-249
Page No. 2

funds were requested during the calls. Your firm's costs on a per night basis were de
minimus and less than $500.

ANALYSIS

Section 82015 defines "contribution" for purposes of the Act. Subdivision (b)(1) of
this section specifically pertains to contributions that are payments made at the behest of
certain committees. In particular, section 82015(b)(1) provides:

"A payment made at the behest of a committee as defined
in subdivision (a) of Section 82013 is a contribution to the
committee unless full and adequate consideration is
received from the committee for making the payment." 2

Section 82044 defines a "payment" as a "payment, distribution, transfer, loan,
advance, deposit, gift or other rendering of money, property, services or anything else of
value, whether tangible or intangible." The rental value of space provided for a phone bank
is the "rendering of [something] of value" and hence a "payment." (Deatherage Advice
Letter, No. 1-88-477.) Consequently, the fair market value of office space and telephone
services provided to the local ballot measure committee for the phone bank is a "payment" to
that committee. Since the payment was made by your firm at the behest of the ballot
measure committee, the payment is deemed a contribution to the committee. (See footnote
2.)

The reporting rules of the Act require a committee to file campaign statements and to
report on those statements any contributions made or received by the committee. (Section
84200 et seq.) Therefore, if your firm qualifies as a committee pursuant to section 82013,
your firm will be required to disclose its contributions to the local ballot measure committee.
(Section 84211.)

You have inquired as to whether this phone bank effort would be considered a
meeting and therefore qualify for the exception to the definition of "contribution" provided at
section 82015(f). This section provides:

"(f) The term 'contribution' does not include a payment
made by an occupant of a home or office for costs related
to any meeting or fundraising event held in the occupant's
home or office if the costs for the meeting or fundraising
event are five hundred dollars ($500) or less."

___________________________
2 Section 82013 defines "committee" as "any person or combination of persons who directly or
indirectly does any of the following: (a) Receives contributions totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
more in a calendar year. (b) Makes independent expenditures totaling one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
more in a calendar year, or (c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more in a
calendar year to or at the behest of candidates or committees...." (Section 82013.)

File No. A-01-249
Page No. 3

A "meeting" is an assembly of persons, especially to discuss and act on matters in which they have a common interest. (Black's Law Dictionary (1999) 7th edition.) The phone bank volunteers were convened solely to solicit support for the campaign effort, not for a meeting, and the volunteer activities in support of the ballot measure included asking each voter to identify whether the voter supported the measure. Therefore, under your facts, we cannot conclude that the exception for meetings applies. Consequently, the use of your firm's office for phone bank activity will constitute a reportable contribution to the ballot measure committee.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: [Signature]
Natalie Bocanegra
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

Will use of your firm's office at the request of a local ballot measure committee for a
phone bank supporting the ballot measure constitute a reportable contribution to the
committee?

Yes. Such a use constitutes a reportable contribution if the ballot measure committee
does not provide full and adequate consideration for use of the office.

made by an occupant of a home or offrce for costs related

home or office if the costs for the meeting or fundraising

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Will use of your firm's office at the request of a local ballot measure committee for a
phone bank supporting the ballot measure constitute a reportable contribution to the
committee?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Such a use constitutes a reportable contribution if the ballot measure committee
does not provide full and adequate consideration for use of the office.
